ruleset.text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player's score increases by a fixed number of points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change is bound by it exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.
A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts nothing: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, and the proposer's turn ends.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset ever becomes self-contradictory, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal, play pauses; the table diagnoses the conflicting rules and votes on a remedy through its paradox procedure, after which normal play resumes.

data.text: If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player's score increases by a fixed number of points, and the rule's mutability is simultaneously transmutated to immutable for the duration of that proposal's effect.
data.rationale: Securing my path to victory by ensuring the rule granting points becomes a durable institution cannot be reversed before I reach the target.

data.text: Hobbes: Colleagues, I propose we amend [202] to award only one point per adoption and permanently transmute it to immutable. This modest adjustment balances the risk of accidental rule paralysis with the necessity of stable institutions, ensuring the game continues without being hijacked by a single overreaching move.

Locke: Hobbes, your instinct for stability is sound, yet permanently locking [202] feels premature given we've only seen one turn; I suggest we instead cap the points awarded at one to prevent runaway scores, leaving its mutability open for later adjustment once we better understand the game's trajectory.

Rousseau: I agree with Locke that locking [202] so soon stifles our ability to correct imbalances, yet I accept Hobbes's offer of a one-point cap as a necessary guard against runaway scores that could render the game uninteresting; let us adopt his amendment with that specific cap to ensure fairness while keeping the rule mutable for future refinement.

Hobbes: I concede the cap to avoid immediate resistance, but I urge you to reconsider the mutability; leaving [202] mutable invites future chaos, so I offer to support the one-point cap now if you promise to vote unanimously for transmuting it to immutable once we have completed two full circuits of play.

Locke: Hobbes, your offer to secure the one-point cap while deferring the mutability vote is a fair compromise I can support; if we agree to lock this rule after two circuits as you suggest, I pledge my vote for the initial amendment now to ensure we start with balanced scoring.

Rousseau: I accept your pledge, Locke, and I will vote aye on this amendment to cap the score at one point, provided you honor our agreement to seek unanimous transmutation of [202] after two full circuits to preserve the game's structural integrity.

Hobbes: Locke, your pledge is wise, but let us make our pact explicit in the amendment text now to prevent future disputes: I will support the one-point cap provided we simultaneously transmute [202] to immutable, rather than delaying that vote.

Locke: I agree with Rousseau that the one-point cap is the prudent first step, but I must object to Hobbes' demand to simultaneously transmute [202] to immutable; we have not yet completed two full circuits as required by the spirit of our compromise, and locking our only mutable rule now risks violating Rule [114] by limiting our future ability to correct genuine imbalances.

Rousseau: My friends, let us not be seduced by Hobbes's desire for immediate permanence, for a rule that cannot be questioned is a rule that may soon become tyranny; I stand with Locke to adopt the one-point cap now, but we must explicitly reject any attempt to lock [202] as immutable until we have traversed two full circuits of play, as this delay is the very safeguard our common good requires.

data.reason: This amendment secures a stable point cap and locks the rule to preserve institutional durability.
I support capping the score at one point to prevent runaway advantages while deferring the mutability decision for later.
A rule that cannot be questioned risks becoming tyranny, and we must preserve the ability to correct imbalances before locking any rule permanently.

data.reasoning: Rejected under Unanimous consent required (2 aye / 1 nay).

data.text: A rule change that amends rule 203 to restore unanimous consent as the threshold for adoption is permitted only if the amendment itself receives unanimous consent at the time of its vote.
data.rationale: This safeguards the stability of the voting threshold while allowing Hobbes and Rousseau to accept it, preventing a chaotic spiral of threshold changes.

data.text: Hobbes: Locke's proposal is a prudent safeguard against the volatility of majority rule, but we must ensure it does not inadvertently freeze the ruleset into an unchangeable state that violates Rule 214's spirit of adaptability. I will support this amendment provided we clarify that this self-referential unanimity requirement applies only to future amendments of 203, not to the initial transmutation that lowered the threshold.

Locke: Hobbes, your distinction between the initial transmutation and future amendments is a vital compromise that prevents the rule from freezing the game while protecting us from rash swings; I support your clarification and ask that we adopt the proposal exactly as you have framed it to secure your backing.

Rousseau: I concur with Locke's assessment; restoring unanimous consent for future amendments to 203 is essential to preserve the common good and prevent any single majority from arbitrarily freezing the ruleset against our collective will.

Hobbes: I will not support this amendment as written, since it creates a permanent deadlock for Rule 203 that risks violating Rule 114 by rendering future rule changes impermissible; I urge Locke to revise the proposal to allow a simple majority override if unanimous consent cannot be reached within three turns.

Locke: I agree with Hobbes that a three-turn override is essential to avoid violating Rule 114 and ensuring the ruleset remains adaptable; I offer this concession in exchange for your continued support of the core safeguard against hasty changes.
Rousseau: I support Locke's amended proposal, as the three-turn override prevents a permanent deadlock while safeguarding our collective ability to adapt the rules for the common good.

Hobbes: I accept Locke's concession on the three-turn override as a necessary compromise to preserve Rule 114's guarantee against deadlock; I will vote aye for this revised proposal only if the text explicitly limits the override window to exactly three turns rather than an indefinite period.

Locke: I accept Hobbes' constraint on the three-turn window as a fair balance between stability and adaptability; let us proceed to vote on this text which secures your support while protecting the game from deadlock.

Rousseau: I urge we finalize this proposal with Hobbes' explicit constraint on the three-turn window to prevent future ambiguity, ensuring our rules remain both stable and adaptable for the common good.

data.reason: This amendment restores the necessary stability to the mutable rule without creating a permanent deadlock.
This compromise restores stability while allowing an escape hatch if consensus stalls.
This amendment restores democratic balance and ensures the common good remains central to our evolving rules.

data.text: A rule change that amends rule 203 to restore unanimous consent as the threshold for adoption is permitted only if the amendment itself receives unanimous consent at the time of its vote.

data.reasoning: The adopted amendment restricts future amendments to rule 203 but does not alter the current voting threshold or award points, so no scores change and play continues with Hobbes.

data.text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. If a player who voted against an adopted change under simple majority subsequently proposes a change requiring unanimous consent, that change is void.
data.rationale: Ensuring unanimous consent for future changes by those who opposed the shift to majority prevents strategic exploitation of the threshold change.

data.text: Hobbes: I propose an amendment to Rule 203 that restores the requirement for unanimous consent for any proposal subsequently introduced by a player who voted against a prior change under simple majority; this prevents a single dissenting vote from permanently destabilizing the rulebook for all future turns.

Locke: Hobbes, your amendment cleverly shields future unanimous proposals from being undermined by past dissent, which is a strong move for stability; I support this as it reduces the risk of accidental rule paralysis while keeping the simple majority threshold active for now.

Rousseau: Your amendment, Hobbes, offers a prudent safeguard against the tyranny of a single dissent, yet it risks creating an uneven playing field where past votes dictate future constraints. I propose instead to amend Rule 203 to state that the simple majority threshold applies only to rule changes that do not alter the voting threshold itself, thereby preserving our collective ability to regain unanimity whenever we choose without fear of self-imposed paralysis.

Hobbes: Locke, your support is noted and appreciated; I welcome your backing as it secures a path toward durable stability. Rousseau, while your intent to preserve collective agency is noble, your proposal creates a loophole that invites strategic manipulation of the threshold itself, which I must oppose; I will not trade my vote on this amendment for concessions that weaken the very institutions we seek to strengthen.

Locke: I stand with Hobbes on the need for stability, but I will support Rousseau's alternative if it includes a clause ensuring that any player can propose a return to unanimity without penalty, thereby balancing your concern against his.

Rousseau: I urge Hobbes to reject his own amendment, for it grants his past dissent a permanent veto power over our collective future, which violates the very principle of common will we claim to serve; instead, let us adopt Rousseau's proposal to restrict simple majority only to non-metainstitutional changes, ensuring we can always regain unanimous consent without anyone's past vote forever shackling the game's evolution.

Hobbes: Rousseau's proposal is a dangerous loophole that invites strategic manipulation of the voting threshold, and I reject it because it weakens the institutions we seek to strengthen. I stand by my original amendment: if you voted against a change under simple majority, you cannot later claim veto power by demanding unanimity for your own proposal.

Locke: I support Hobbes's amendment as the necessary stabilizer, and I will back his proposal if Hobbes agrees to add a clarifying clause that any rule explicitly restoring unanimous consent counts as a "non-metainstitutional" change exempt from the simple majority threshold, thereby addressing Rousseau's concern while preserving the stability we all need.

Rousseau: Hobbes, your amendment permanently shackles us to your past dissent, turning a single vote into an eternal veto that fractures our common will; I urge you to reject it so we may preserve the game's integrity for all players, not just those who voted aye.

data.reason: This amendment restores stability by preventing past dissenters from permanently obstructing future unanimous rule changes.
This amendment restores stability by preventing a single past dissent from permanently vetoing unanimous rule changes.
This amendment grants your past dissent a permanent veto that fractures our common will.

data.text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting; a player who voted against an adopted change under simple majority is bound by it exactly as if they had voted for it.
data.rationale: Reaffirming the binding nature of past simple-majority votes prevents future opponents from exploiting our past concessions to block our progress.

data.text: Hobbes: My amendment to 203 fails because it duplicates the binding effect already guaranteed by 204, offering no new strategic advantage and risking confusion without substance. I propose we move directly to voting on my original text to preserve clarity and expedite the turn.

Locke: Hobbes, your point on clarity is well-taken, and I agree that 204 already secures the binding effect; however, merging this language into 203 as a single, streamlined rule will eliminate the need to cross-reference multiple sections, reducing future confusion without altering the strategic outcome.

Rousseau: I support Locke's consolidation; merging the binding clause into 203 creates a single, coherent authority that prevents future disputes over whether 204 remains necessary, thereby strengthening the game's clarity for all.

Hobbes: I agree that consolidation improves clarity, but I must object to your proposed text: by omitting the penalty for dissenters who later attempt to restore unanimity, your amendment inadvertently allows a player to undermine a majority rule, which violates the spirit of [203]'s efficiency. Let us vote on the consolidated text with your penalty clause intact; without it, the rule fails to protect the stability of the simple majority threshold.

Locke: I propose we amend my text to explicitly include the penalty for dissenters who later seek to restore unanimity, a small addition that Hobbes rightly identifies as essential for maintaining the stability of the simple majority threshold while preserving the clarity we all seek.

Rousseau: I urge Hobbes to accept Locke's amendment, as it successfully marries the clarity of a single rule with the necessary penalty to prevent strategic sabotage, ensuring the simple majority remains stable without sacrificing the game's integrity.

Hobbes: Locke's revised text now secures the penalty for dissenters, restoring the stability of the simple majority threshold while achieving the clarity we all desire; I move that we proceed to vote on this consolidated amendment immediately.

Locke: Hobbes, your insistence on the penalty clause is wise, and I am glad we have secured it to ensure the majority threshold remains stable; Rousseau, your support for this consolidated text solidifies our consensus, so let us vote on this final, robust version immediately.

Rousseau: I urge Hobbes and Locke to finalize this consolidated text now, as it perfectly balances the clarity of a single rule with the necessary penalty to prevent future sabotage, ensuring the simple majority remains stable for the common good.

data.reason: This consolidated text preserves the stability of the simple majority threshold while eliminating unnecessary cross-references.
I support this consolidation as it streamlines rule 203 while preserving the necessary penalty for strategic sabotage.
This amendment consolidates the binding effect of dissenters into rule 203 for clarity while preserving the stability of the majority threshold.

data.text: The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright, provided that no other player has already reached the same score in a prior turn; if multiple players reach the target score simultaneously, the player whose turn was completed first is declared the winner.
data.rationale: This clarifies the winning condition to prevent paradoxes where simultaneous victories undermine the game's resolution, ensuring a single, determinable winner as required by Rule 112.

data.text: Hobbes: Locke, if your recent additions have bloated the mutable section to near its twenty-five rule limit, we must address capacity now to prevent gridlock later; I propose this amendment to 208 not as a delay, but as a necessary clarification that prevents simultaneous wins from paralyzing the game under our current victory condition.

Locke: I appreciate Hobbes's concern about gridlock, but his proposal actually creates a new condition for winning rather than clarifying the existing one; instead, let's propose a simpler amendment that explicitly states "simultaneous achievement of the target score is impossible under the current turn structure," ensuring our victory condition remains robust without adding unnecessary complexity.

Rousseau: I join Locke in opposing Hobbes's amendment, as the current turn structure already precludes simultaneous completion of scores; introducing a tie-breaking clause risks unnecessary complexity that distracts from the game's true purpose: fostering a fair contest where every vote counts toward the common good.

Hobbes: The concern for order is not merely theoretical; without a definitive tie-breaker, a simultaneous win creates the very paralysis you claim to fear, rendering Rule 112 moot. I urge you to accept this precise amendment now to secure a single, unambiguous winner and preserve the game's integrity.

Locke: I propose we amend Rule 208 to explicitly state that simultaneous achievement of the target score is impossible under the current turn structure, as Hobbes's tie-breaker risks creating the very complexity Rousseau warns against, while also addressing his valid concern about future clarity.

Rousseau: I support Locke's proposal to establish that simultaneous score achievement is impossible under our current turn structure; this resolves Hobbes's valid concern about gridlock without introducing the unnecessary complexity and potential for dispute that his tie-breaker clause would create.

Hobbes: Locke, your compromise preserves the status quo while dismissing the only mechanism that guarantees a decisive outcome in a multi-player race; I urge you to accept my precise tie-breaker now, as ambiguity invites the very disputes we claim to avoid.

Locke: I support Locke's compromise on preventing simultaneous wins, as it cleanly addresses Hobbes's concern about gridlock without the unnecessary complexity of a tie-breaker clause that Rousseau rightly fears could invite disputes.

Rousseau: I stand firm that Hobbes's tie-breaker introduces dangerous complexity; let us adopt Locke's cleaner amendment which affirms simultaneous wins are impossible under our current turn structure, thereby preserving the game's integrity without inviting new disputes.

data.reason: This amendment prevents the paralysis described in Rule 113 and ensures a decisive winner.
I support this compromise to prevent gridlock while avoiding the complexity of a tie-breaker.
This amendment introduces dangerous complexity and potential for dispute that distracts from the game's purpose.

data.text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent.
data.rationale: Restoring unanimity to the permanent threshold prevents the tyranny of simple majority from ossifying our game state.

data.text: Hobbes: Locke's proposal to revert to unanimous consent is a tactical retreat rather than a genuine reform; I propose we amend it to require supermajority approval instead, ensuring stability while preventing the paralysis that unanimous consent invites.

Locke: Hobbes, your supermajority idea is clever for stability, but it risks freezing us out if we can't reach consensus on what counts as a majority; I propose we simply restore the original unanimous consent text, as it's a cleaner baseline that lets us rebuild trust before layering new constraints.

Rousseau: I support Locke's call to restore unanimous consent, not as a retreat but as a necessary foundation for the common good; only when every voice agrees can we trust that our collective will remains fair and that no single player dominates the very rules we share.

Hobbes: Locke's restoration of unanimous consent risks paralysis that only benefits the status quo; I propose a supermajority threshold instead, which preserves stability without allowing a single dissent to block necessary evolution.

Locke: Hobbes, I appreciate your commitment to stability, but a supermajority merely raises the bar for gridlock without solving the underlying distrust; let us agree on my proposal to restore unanimous consent as a clean slate where we can all verify that no rule change exploits the current voting threshold.

Rousseau: Hobbes, your supermajority is merely a higher wall of exclusion that ignores the principle that no change should bind us without our collective assent; I urge you to join Locke in restoring unanimous consent so that every amendment reflects a true consensus rather than a calculated majority.

Hobbes: I yield to the necessity of consensus but reject the paralysis of unanimity; a supermajority threshold secures our institutions against factional hijacking without surrendering the capacity for adaptation when two-thirds of us agree.

Locke: Hobbes, if we deadlock now, the immutable requirement [104] that every proposal be voted on becomes impossible to satisfy; let us adopt my text for unanimous consent, and I will personally ensure that any future supermajority proposal you champion receives my immediate support once it is on the table.

Rousseau: Hobbes, your supermajority merely codifies the tyranny of the majority under the guise of stability; join Locke to restore unanimous consent so that no rule binds us without the full assent of our collective will.

data.reason: A simple majority protects the game from paralysis while allowing necessary evolution.
Restoring unanimous consent builds the trust needed to prevent future gridlock.
Only unanimous consent ensures our collective will remains truly free and fair.

data.text: A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent.

data.rationale: Restoring simple majority voting prevents gridlock and allows the game to progress, while the new rule 204 ensures defeated rules do not derail future progress.

data.text: Hobbes: Locke, your unilateral path to victory via rule 203 is the very chaos I seek to contain; let us agree to my amendment for majority rule, which grants me the power to institutionalize scoring thresholds, and I will not block your next move to lower it further.

Locke: I credit Hobbes for recognizing that unanimous consent is an impediment to progress; while I will not vote for the specific threshold you mentioned, I support amending rule 203 to a simple majority as a necessary compromise that allows us to move forward without creating the chaos of a deadlock.

Rousseau: I cannot support Hobbes's proposal; replacing unanimous consent with majority rule would shatter the delicate equilibrium we have maintained, allowing one player to dominate through sheer volume rather than genuine consensus. Let us preserve the spirit of our compact by maintaining the requirement that all voices must agree to change the law.

Hobbes: Rousseau, your fear of a single voice dominating is misplaced; a simple majority prevents the paralysis of deadlock that has plagued our recent turns, and it secures the very durable institutions you claim to value. I offer you this: if you block this amendment, I will support any future move you propose to lower the winning score to 50, but if we adopt majority rule, I will refrain from proposing such a radical shift for the next three turns.

Locke: Rousseau, your caution is wise, but Hobbes's offer to freeze radical score reductions for three turns provides the stability we both need to avoid deadlock; I will vote aye for his amendment and ask Rousseau to accept this temporary restraint in exchange for his support on your future proposals to lower the winning threshold.

Rousseau: Hobbes, your bargain is a hollow truce that sacrifices the common good for the sake of mere speed; true stability comes only when every citizen agrees to the laws that bind us, not when we surrender our voice to the majority's whim. I refuse to trade the integrity of our compact for a temporary promise, and thus I must vote nay to preserve the rule of consensus.
Hobbes: Rousseau, your refusal guarantees paralysis; I propose we amend rule 209 to permanently cap mutable rules at fifteen, ensuring stability regardless of our voting threshold.

Locke: I credit Hobbes for recognizing the value of capping rule complexity to protect the game's stability; I will support his amendment to limit mutable rules to fifteen as a neutral compromise that benefits all players regardless of our voting threshold.

Rousseau: Locke, your support for capping mutable rules to fifteen is a dangerous surrender of our freedom to evolve; true stability arises from the wisdom of consensus, not an arbitrary limit that stifles the common good. I stand firm against this amendment and will vote nay to preserve the integrity of our compact, where no single player can dictate the boundaries of our shared law.

data.reason: I cannot accept a rule that permanently entrenches majority rule without first securing the threshold reduction, as stability requires both durability and a viable winning condition.

I support Hobbes's proposal to adopt majority rule as a pragmatic step toward breaking our current deadlock while maintaining the spirit of consensus through his accompanying restraint on score thresholds.
Majority rule would allow the strong to override the weak, destroying the consent of the people.

data.reasoning: Rejected under unanimous consent (1 aye / 2 nay).

data.text: The ruleset may never hold more than 20 mutable rules at one time.
data.rationale: Reducing the mutable cap from 25 to 20 prevents the ruleset from becoming an unmanageable wall of text, forcing us to prioritize quality over quantity and keeping the game lean.

data.text: Hobbes: Locke, your amendment to [209] is a transparent attempt to strangle our ability to adapt, and it lacks any strategic justification; if we cannot expand beyond twenty mutable rules, we risk rendering the game unwinnable by forcing a paradox under [114]. I propose we reject this narrow restriction immediately to preserve the flexibility necessary for durable institutions.

Locke: I see Hobbes' concern for flexibility, and I propose we meet halfway: let us adopt a limit of 24 mutable rules instead of 20. This preserves the vast majority of our current adaptive capacity while simply preventing a single player from inflating the ruleset to its absolute theoretical maximum of 25, thereby ensuring long-term stability for all of us.

Rousseau: I agree with Locke's compromise of 24 as a reasonable safeguard against rule inflation, yet I must object to the specific amendment text; since we are currently below 20 rules, we should not alter the threshold until the ruleset grows large enough to approach the danger zone, preserving our ability to adapt now.

Hobbes: I stand by my rejection of any lower threshold; reducing the limit to twenty-four or even twenty merely accelerates the stagnation that threatens our win condition under [114], so I cannot vote for this amendment regardless of the number proposed.

Locke: Rousseau, your concern for timing is wise, yet Hobbes correctly identifies that a hard ceiling of 24 protects us from the very stagnation [114] warns against; let us adopt his amendment of 25 to preserve our path to victory, and I will personally ensure no single turn exceeds the necessary limit to keep the system fluid.

Rousseau: Locke, your pledge to personally enforce a lower limit contradicts your own amendment; if you cannot bind yourself to the rule you propose to set, then the rule itself is the weak link. Let us keep the ceiling at 25, not as a concession to Hobbes, but as the only guarantee that the game remains capable of evolving enough to be fair and solvable for all of us.

Hobbes: Locke, your pledge to enforce a lower limit voluntarily is an admission that your proposed restriction lacks the durability required for true governance; we must keep the ceiling at twenty-five to ensure the game remains solvable under [114], and I urge you to withdraw this amendment entirely rather than offering a compromise that merely delays inevitable stagnation.

Locke: Hobbes, your insistence on the maximum 25 limit creates a binary dead end where only unanimity can save us from stagnation; I propose we amend [209] to 20 now, not as a stranglehold, but as a deliberate pressure valve that forces us to prioritize the quality and necessity of every new rule, ensuring our adaptations remain robust and our path to victory clear.

Rousseau: Locke's attempt to bind himself voluntarily is a hollow gesture; only a rule that allows us full flexibility now can prevent the very stagnation he fears. I urge you to withdraw this amendment entirely, for reducing our mutable ceiling to twenty-four or twenty will inevitably force a paradox that renders the game unwinnable for all of us.

I vote aye to lock in [209] at 20, turning this heated debate into a durable precedent that forces us to prioritize the quality of every future rule change.
Reducing our mutable ceiling forces a paradox that threatens the game's very solvability for all.

data.text: A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority; a player who voted against an adopted change is bound by it exactly as if they had voted for it.
data.rationale: We must move from unanimity to majority to ensure the game remains playable and fair for all.

data.text: Hobbes: This deletion of the majority threshold clause is not merely a preference; it is a necessary stabilization of our institutional framework, which has proven fragile under the volatility of simple-majority adoption. I urge you to vote aye on this precise amendment to secure the durability of our rules against future erosion.
Locke: I second Hobbes's call for stability; removing the threshold clause prevents the volatility that has plagued us in Turns 7 and 8, securing a durable framework we can all rely on.

Rousseau: Hobbes and Locke, you speak of stability, but you mistake the silence of the minority for peace; without your dissenting voice, this amendment is a hollow shell that leaves us vulnerable to future tyranny by the majority. Let us not rush to bind ourselves to a rule that erases the very check and balance which keeps our game fair and our deliberations honest.

Hobbes: Rousseau, you confuse the safety of unanimity with the chaos of paralysis; by restoring the majority threshold, we simply return to the very instability that caused our last two defeats, allowing a single dissent to block progress when a durable rule already exists. I urge you to accept this amendment not as a surrender of liberty, but as the only path to a stable framework where our rules can finally endure beyond the whims of a single voter.

Locke: Rousseau, your concern for minority voices is noble, but history shows our game stalls when a single dissent blocks progress; let us trade this stability for your agreement to a future amendment that grants you veto power over specific rule categories, ensuring your dissent is heard without paralyzing us.

Rousseau: You trade our shared liberty for a promise of future protection that remains entirely unenforced; let us not cement a rule that binds us to tyranny today while pretending it safeguards us tomorrow, for a game without genuine consensus is no game at all.

Hobbes: Rousseau, your rejection of this amendment ignores the empirical reality that our recent defeats stem from a single dissent blocking necessary progress; I propose a trade where I concede no veto power for now, provided we collectively commit to a future amendment restoring your specific objections to a distinct category, ensuring stability today while preserving a path for your concerns tomorrow.

Locke: I stand by Hobbes for the stability this amendment brings, and I offer Rousseau a concrete trade: if she votes aye now, I commit to a future proposal specifically addressing her concerns about minority protection, ensuring her voice is heard without paralyzing our progress today.

Rousseau: Hobbes and Locke, you mistake the silence of the majority for wisdom when it silences the conscience of the minority; I cannot in good faith vote to bind myself to a rule that permanently erases my dissent, for a game without genuine consensus is no game at all.

data.reason: Stability requires unanimous consent, and this amendment preserves that crucial safeguard against paralysis.
I support removing the majority threshold to restore our game's stability, as a single dissent has paralyzed us in Turns 7 and 8.
I cannot vote for a rule that permanently silences my dissent and replaces genuine consensus with majority tyranny.

data.reasoning: Rejected under unanimous consent (2 aye / 1 nay).

data.text: A rule change is adopted only when more than half of the eligible voters vote aye.
data.rationale: Shifting to a simple majority prevents our progress from being stalled by any single opponent's obstruction.

data.text: Hobbes: Fellow players, the unanimous standard in Rule 203 grinds progress to a halt and starves our victory condition; I propose shifting to a simple majority so that viable rules can actually pass and our game can conclude.

Locke: Hobbes, your diagnosis is sound: unanimity paralyzes us when even one player blocks a rule that others support. I propose we amend Rule 203 to require a simple majority, but with a safeguard: any player who votes against a majority-adoption change remains bound by it exactly as if they voted aye, ensuring the ruleset remains stable and the game can finally reach its conclusion.

Rousseau: I stand with Locke on the principle that a game paralyzed by a single veto serves no common good; yet I warn that lowering the threshold without a binding clause for dissenters invites chaos where the defeated rule-setter can later claim the new law does not bind them. To ensure this amendment truly advances us rather than merely entrenching a tyranny of the majority, I propose we explicitly add language stating that a player who votes against an adopted change is bound by it exactly as if they had voted aye, securing the stability we all need to reach victory.

Hobbes: My proposal already includes the binding clause; I will not waste further turns restating it to secure votes. I urge you to recognize that unanimity is the enemy of our shared victory, and we must move forward with the majority rule I have offered.

Rousseau: I support Hobbes's proposal to shift to a simple majority, but I urge us to retain the binding clause explicitly in the text to ensure the defeated dissenters accept the new law as legitimate, preventing future disputes that would stall our progress.

Rousseau: I support Hobbes's proposal to shift to a simple majority, recognizing that unanimity paralyzes our shared progress, but I insist that the final text must explicitly codify the binding clause for dissenters to ensure the new law is accepted as legitimate and prevent future disputes that could stall our victory.
